text: The surgeon identifies the permanent suture securing the gastric neurostimulator leads to the greater curvature of the stomach and removes adhesions to restore normal anatomical configuration. The jejunostomy tube site is visible approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, and the surgeon uses a stapler to detach the small bowel from the abdominal wall without narrowing the intestinal lumen.
The surgeon identifies and prepares to repair a small hiatal hernia, which was previously detected during preoperative endoscopy.

The surgeon creates a second gastrotomy for anvil placement, observing a moderate amount of gastric fluid due to gastroparesis. Dense adhesions are present posterior to the stomach, and a finger dissector is used to create a tunnel for the stapler. The gastric stimulator leads are positioned near the GJ anastomosis site, but the Rulim is brought up medially to the leads for anastomosis without needing a gastric wedge resection.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative endoscopy to evaluate the gastric pouch and conduct a leak test. The omentum is mobilized and positioned beneath the gastric stimulator leads to prevent them from resting on top of the Roux limb. The final view shows the gastric stimulator and the Roux limb in their respective positions.
